coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of tetracycline in wastewater based on heteroatoms-doped reduced state carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Qing
Long, Caicheng
Qin, Lingfeng
Jiang, Zixin
Qing, Taiping
Zhang, Peng
Feng, Bo
coredata.dc:description: A large amount of tetracycline (TC) persists in water, soil, food, and feed due to the overuse of antibiotics, causing serious environmental problems such as damage to ecosystems and posing risks to human health. Thus, there is an urgent need to find a method to detect TC that is practical, rapid, sensitive, and offers ready visualization of TC levels so that adequate remediation measures can be immediately implemented. Herein, we report a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode nanosensor for the detection of TC based on reduced state carbon dots (r-CDs). In the presence of TC, the emission fluorescence of r-CDs was quenched by the Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism to achieve high-sensitivity detection of TC with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.73 nM. Moreover, TC was also detected by r-CDs via a noticeable color change of the solution (from colorless to red) with the naked eye, having an LOD of 0.46 μM. Furthermore, r-CDs have excellent selectivity and sensitivity in detecting TC in wastewater, and therefore, have practical applications in wastewater treatment. The fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode proposed in this work may offer a unique idea for the detection of TC, with great prospects for environmental wastewater applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline detection
Carbon dots
Fluorescent and colorimetric
Dual-mode
Nanosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield/lifetime
Detection of TC with the r-CDs
Optimization of synthesis conditions of r-CDs
Fluorescence sensing of TC based on the r-CDs
Colorimetric sensing of TC based on the r-CDs
Possible mechanism of the detection to TC of r-CDs
Detection of TC in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 21777135 , 51708475 ) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 2019JJ40283 , 2019JJ50596 , 2018JJ3496 ), the Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department, China ( 19A501 , 18C0102 ), and Hunan 2011 Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Engineering & Technology with Environmental Benignity and Effective Resource Utilization .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (the inset is the corresponding lattice spacing) of the r-CDs (a); particle size distribution histograms of the r-CDs (b); FT-IR spectra of the pristine CDs and r-CDs (c); XPS patterns of the pristine CDs and r-CDs (d); high-resolution XPS spectra of P 2p of the pristine CDs and r-CDs. (e), C 1s of the pristine CDs and r-CDs (f).

UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectrum of the r-CDs, (Ex/Em = 320/450 nm), inset: emission photograph of the r-CDs under the sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right) (a). FL spectra at different excitation wavelengths (Ex = 280–470 nm, Em = 425–525 nm) (b), 3D-contour plots of excitation/emission spectra (Ex/Em = 320/450 nm) (c), and variation of FL intensity in 7 days (Ex/Em = 320/450 nm) (d) of the r-CDs.

FL emission spectra of the 300 μg/mL r-CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of TC from 0 to 600 μM (a). Relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration of TC, where F0 and F are FL intensities of the r-CDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of TC, respectively (b). FL intensity response (F/F0) of 300 μg/mL r-CDs toward various metal ions (Hg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Li+, 100 μM; red bars) and the subsequent addition of 100 μM TC (blue bars) (c). FL intensity response (F/F0) of the 300 μg/mL r-CDs toward various antibiotics (TC, CTE, OXY, AZM, CHL, AMX, LEX, and STR, 100 μM; red bars) and the subsequent addition of 100 μM TC (blue bars) (d), where F0 and F are FL intensities of the r-CDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of TC, respectively. (Ex/Em = 320/450 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Absorbance spectra of the r-CDs and NaBH4 in the absence and presence of TC (a). Absorbance spectra of the 300 μg/mL CDs, r-CDs in the absence and presence of 100 μM TC (b). Absorbance spectra of the 300 μg/mL r-CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of TC from 0 to 600 μM, inset: photographs of r-CD with the addition of different concentrations of TC (from left to right: 0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, and 600 μM) under sunlight (c). Relationship between absorbance (at 537 nm) and the concentration of TC (d). Absorbance response (at 537 nm) of 300 μg/mL r-CDs toward various metal ions (Hg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Li+, 100 μM; red bars) and the subsequent addition of 100 μM TC (blue bars) (e). Absorbance response at 537 nm of the 300 μg/mL r-CDs toward various antibiotics (TC, CTE, OXY, AZM, CHL, AMX, LEX, and STR, 100 μM; red bars) and the subsequent addition of 100 μM TC (blue bars) (f). (Absorbance at 537 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Stern–Volmer plot for binding of TC to the 300 μg/mL r-CDs (Ex/Em = 320/450 nm), where F0 and F are FL intensities of the r-CDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of TC, respectively (a). Fluorescence emission spectra of r-CDs (black curve, λex = 320 nm) and absorption spectra of the TC (red curve) (b). Fluorescence decay curves of r-CDs at 450 nm before and after addition of TC, inset: the calculated average fluorescence decay time of r-CDs at 450 nm before and after adding TC (c). UV–vis absorption spectra of 300 μg/mL r-CDs with (300 μM) different antibiotics (d). FT-IR spectra of r-CDs before and after addition of 100 μM TC (e). high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s of r-CDs after adding 100 μM TC (f). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram for the synthesis of r-CDs and its sensing performance in a fluorescent (Ex/Em = 320/450 nm)/colorimetric (at 537 nm) manner.
Recovery rate of TC in wastewater by fluorescent and colorimetric detection.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This paper has been recommended for acceptance by Charles Wong.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of tetracycline in wastewater based on heteroatoms-doped reduced state carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, 411105, Hunan Province, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, 411105, Hunan Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A large amount of tetracycline (TC) persists in water, soil, food, and feed due to the overuse of antibiotics, causing serious environmental problems such as damage to ecosystems and posing risks to human health. Thus, there is an urgent need to find a method to detect TC that is practical, rapid, sensitive, and offers ready visualization of TC levels so that adequate remediation measures can be immediately implemented. Herein, we report a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode nanosensor for the detection of TC based on reduced state carbon dots (r-CDs). In the presence of TC, the emission fluorescence of r-CDs was quenched by the Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism to achieve high-sensitivity detection of TC with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.73 nM. Moreover, TC was also detected by r-CDs via a noticeable color change of the solution (from colorless to red) with the naked eye, having an LOD of 0.46 μM. Furthermore, r-CDs have excellent selectivity and sensitivity in detecting TC in wastewater, and therefore, have practical applications in wastewater treatment. The fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode proposed in this work may offer a unique idea for the detection of TC, with great prospects for environmental wastewater applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most commonly used antibiotic, tetracycline (TC) has been employed to treat various bacterial infections in humans, animals, and aquaculture owing to its broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, good oral absorption, low toxicity, and low cost (Dong et al., 2020; Lipsitch et al., 2002). Recent reports indicate that veterinary TC is commonly incorporated into animal feed to improve growth rate and feed efficiency. However, due to their low bioavailability, 20%–75% of the ingested TC is mainly excreted into the environment through urine and feces (Zhang et al., 2015), causing high residues of TC and its metabolites to be detected in soil and swine wastewater (Hamscher et al., 2002; Ji et al., 2012). For example, the concentration of TC ranged from 0 mg/mL to 300 mg/mL in swine wastewater (Dong et al., 2020), and in the river reaches it reached 1730.1 ng/L (Zhang et al., 2014b). The presence of TC in the environment can cause antibiotic resistance and seriously threaten human and animal health. Many analytical methods have been developed for monitoring TC, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Hamscher et al., 2002; Zhang et al., 2014b), electrochemical analysis (Qiao et al., 2018), fluorescence (Tang et al., 2019), and chemiluminescence (Jalalian et al., 2018). Nevertheless, these approaches with high sensitivity can only be conducted in well-equipped laboratories because they require professional technicians, expensive laboratory instruments, and complex sample pretreatments (Zhang and Chen, 2016). Thus, the development of an environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and portable sensor for TC detection in complicated real water samples is important, although challenging.

Carbon dots (CDs) have received considerable attention for environmental pollutant detection owing to their abundant and inexpensive raw materials, outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, and easy functionalization (Zheng et al., 2020). Doping CDs with different heteroatoms (X-CDs) may modulate their properties, which can offer opportunities for their applications in environmental pollution control and remediation (Zhou et al., 2017). Therefore, CDs are expected to replace traditional fluorescent dyes and quantum dots for the detection of environmental pollutants. For example, Yang et al. synthesized CDs with L-cysteine and phosphorus pentoxide whose fluorescence is sensitive to tetracycline antibiotics (including tetracycline, oxytetracycline, minocycline, methacycline, demeclocycline, and chlortetracycline), and then developed tetracycline antibiotics detection platform (Yang et al., 2014a). Most of the current detection methods based on CDs detect tetracycline antibiotics, but not TC. Further, the interference of similar tetracycline antibiotics limits the specific detection of TC. Most sensing platforms based on CDs also employ a single emission off-on fluorescent (FL) intensity for the output signal. However, these approaches encounter some difficulties in practical applications because the FL intensity can be easily affected by various external conditions, such as probe concentration, excitation light intensity, detection environment, and instrument efficiency (Han et al., 2020; Long et al., 2021a; Sun et al., 2019). In this case, the dual-mode detection method can eliminate the FL intensity fluctuations caused by the above-mentioned factors when compared with the single-mode detection method, realizing high-sensitivity detection and maintaining favorable selectivity (Han et al., 2020; Long et al., 2021a). For instance, based on red B, N, S-CDs, Liu et al. performed colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode for the detection of Fe3+ in biological samples (Sun et al., 2019). The dual-mode sensor can provide fluorescence detection with high sensitivity as well as results visible to the naked eye. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode nanosensor to detect TC independently.

In this work, we used sucrose, phosphoric acid, and ethylenediamine as raw materials to prepare pristine CDs. Then, we synthesized the N–, P–, and B– co-doped reduced state carbon dots (r-CDs) via a simple reduction pathway, resulting in the CO on the surface being reduced to C–OH. The r-CDs can not only be solely employed for the detection of TC as a fluorescence probe, but can also be easily observed through obvious color change (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence of r-CDs based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) was strongly quenched by the addition of TC. Simultaneously, TC concentrations can also be readily visualized through a significant color change (from colorless to red) in sunlight owing to the specific coordination between r-CDs and TC. Overall, this work provides a new strategy for the highly sensitive and visible detection of TC in wastewater, and offers a method for the design of dual-mode sensing mechanisms based on novel stimuli-responsive materials for different analytical purposes.

Sucrose, phosphoric acid, ethylenediamine, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and antibiotics such as tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTE), oxytetracycline (OXY), azithromycin (AZM), chloramphenicol (CHL), amoxicillin (AMX), cephalexin (LEX), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), and streptomycin sulfate (STR) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fe(NO3)3, KNO3, NaHCO3, CuSO4, CoCl2, MnSO4, ZnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, HgCl2, MgSO4, CaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, BaCl2, and Al(NO3)3 were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were analytically pure and were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (UPW) was used throughout the experiment. 1000 Da membranes used for material purification were obtained from Sangon Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Material filtration was carried out using a 0.22 μm membrane (Polyethersulfone, PES) purchased from Tianjin Jinteng Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the r-CDs were obtained using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, America). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on an FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet) equipped with 210–230 V and 50–60 Hz. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a multifunctional photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) equipped with 1486.6 eV Al Kα rays and 12 kV. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption measurements were obtained using a UV-521 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) employing a 5/5 nm slit width with a Xe lamp. Zeta potential images were obtained using a ZEN 3700 nanoparticle size potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). Fluorescence lifetime was measured using fluorescence steady-state/transient spectrometers (FLS 1000, Edinburgh). Photographs were taken using a Nikon camera (D 3500).

First, sucrose (6 g) and phosphoric acid (6 mL) were dissolved in 18 mL of ultrapure water and heated for 50 min at 80 °C in a thermostatic water bath to obtain a brown liquid. Then, 18 mL of ethylenediamine was added while it was hot and fully reacted to obtain a dark brown liquid, which was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate the unreacted substances. The filtrate was dialyzed for 72 h to obtain carbon dots solution which was added to excess NaBH4 and stirred gently for 48 h at 25 °C to obtain r-CDs. The excess reductant was removed by heating at 80 °C followed by dialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield/lifetime
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of r-CDs has been reported in previous studies (Alivisatos, 1996; Liang et al., 2013). Quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54, 0.1 mol L−1, η = 1.33) was used as the standard and the excitation wavelength (320 nm) as the reference (Kwon and Rhee, 2012; Liu et al., 2007). The specific calculation is shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
Y x
=
Y s
F x
A s
η x 2
F s
A x
η s 2
where F, A, and η represent FL intensity, absorbance, and refractive index, respectively. The subscripts “s” and “x” indicate known quinine sulfate and r-CDs, respectively.

The fluorescence lifetime spectrum was recorded on an FLS 1000 fluorescence steady-state/transient spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, and the average fluorescence lifetimes were computed using Eq. (2).
(2)
τ =
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
+
B 3
τ 3 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3
where
τ
represents the average lifetimes of the r-CDs,
τ 1
(
τ 2
,
τ 3
)
represents the fitted fluorescence lifetime, and
B 1
(
B 2
,
B 3
)
represents the corresponding proportion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC with the r-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescent and colorimetric response of r-CDs to TC, various concentrations of TC (10 μL each, 0–600 μM) were added to a mixture of 500 μL of r-CDs solution (1.8 mg/mL) and 2.49 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 10.0). The total volume of the solution system was 3 mL. The emission spectra of the mixture solution were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer after a reaction time of 2 min. The absorbance was measured using a UV spectrophotometer after 70 min of incubation at 25 °C. The color changes of the mixture were observed, and photographs were taken using a digital camera.

To further explore the selectivity of r-CDs detection for TC, the selective experiments of r-CDs toward TC were conducted via adding various antibiotic solutions (chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTE), oxytetracycline (OXY), azithromycin (AZM), chloramphenicol (CHL), amoxicillin (AMX), cephalexin (LEX), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), and streptomycin sulfate (STR)) instead of TC and analyzing the response of the fluorescent and colorimetric modes. The anti-interference experiments of our designed sensor were conducted in the presence of some interfering substances often found in wastewater, such as metal ions (Hg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Li+) or other antibiotics (as mentioned above). Because of the complexity of the concentration of interfering substances in the environment, we also set different ratios (TC/interfering substances) as 1:1, 1:2, and 1:4. Then, the FL intensity was measured after 2 min, the change in color was observed under sunlight after incubating for 70 min at 25 °C, and the absorbance was recorded.

TC was detected in swine wastewater samples collected from the Xian Feng Pig Farm (Xiangtan, China). First, the sample was centrifuged (8000 rpm min−1, 10 min) and filtered using 0.22 μm membrane filtration for further purification. Before performing TC detection, we used high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) for detection on a real water sample. Different concentrations of the standard TC solution were added to the water sample. Then, the FL spectra of the water samples were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the absorbance spectra were measured using a UV spectrophotometer after incubating for 70 min. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions of r-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized from sucrose, phosphoric acid, and ethylenediamine, and the optimization of time, temperature, and reactant ratio have been detailed in a previous study (Huang et al., 2018). In this work, the concentration of NaBH4, stirring time, and temperature were optimized to prepare r-CDs that met the best conditions for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection. Under the influence of the above stated reaction conditions, the concentration of NaBH4 and the stirring temperature had little influence on the FL intensity of the r-CDs, which mainly influenced the absorbance of the mixture (Fig. S1). Based on the optimizations conducted, the requirements for the best absorbance and FL intensity were met simultaneously. In subsequent experiments, the optimal conditions for preparing r-CDs were the addition of 10 mg/mL NaBH4 and stirring at 25 °C for 48 h.

The particle size distribution and morphology of the r-CDs were measured by HRTEM. The images gathered indicate that the prepared r-CDs were spherical and dispersed without apparent aggregation (Fig. 1 a). The lattice spacing of r-CDs was 0.20 nm (inset in Fig. 1a). The average size of r-CDs was approximately 1.9 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1b) (Yang et al., 2011). Compared with the pristine CDs, the particle size and physical properties of the r-CDs showed little change (Fig. S2). The structure and functional groups of the r-CDs were characterized by FT-IR. The r-CDs exhibited the typical peaks at 3441.0 cm−1, and 2922.0 cm−1, representing the O–H and C–H bonds, respectively (Huang et al., 2018; Long et al., 2020; Shangguan et al., 2017). The peaks at 1384.3 cm−1 and 1098.0 cm−1 were assigned to N–H and C–N bonds, respectively (Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2014a), indicating that nitrogen was presented in r-CDs. In addition, the stretching vibrations of the CC and C–C bonds resulted in peaks at 1445.7 cm−1 and 937.7 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, the absorption peak at 528.7 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of PO4 3−, confirming the successful introduction of phosphorus atoms into the r-CDs (Fig. 1c) (Bogireddy et al., 2020; Gong et al., 2016a). The surface of the original CDs was rich in carbonyl groups (CO). After reduction by NaBH4 (Fig. 1c), the CO groups on the CDs were reduced to C–OH. Additionally, the –OH group on the surface of the r-CDs had a strong reducing ability (Fig. 1f and S3) (Xu et al., 2020).

The elemental analysis and surface chemical bonds of the r-CDs were further explored using XPS. The r-CDs showed six typical peaks at 531.22, 399.17, 284.86, 191.64, 137.45, and 82.79 eV (Fig. 1d), corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, B1s, P2s, and P2p, respectively. Compared with the pristine CDs, the r-CDs had a new addition of boron atoms (Fig. 1d). Moreover, r-CDs were mainly comprised of oxygen (42.22%), carbon (25.12%), boron (14.69%), nitrogen (3.61%), and phosphorus (15.07%), as revealed by XPS elemental analysis. More detailed information about the chemical bond species present on the r-CDs was explored in the single-element-fitting XPS energy spectrum. Two binding energy peaks at 132.6 eV and 134.8 eV represented the bands of P–O and P–C/P–N in the P 2p fitting spectrum of r-CDs (Fig. 1e) (Shangguan et al., 2017). The homologous N 1s spectra are shown in Fig. S4, in which four binding energy peaks at 398.6, 400.0, 403.3, and 407.1 eV indicate C–N–C, N–H, N–(C)3, and N–N/P–N bonds, respectively. The high resolution B 1s spectrum also exhibited two peaks at 191.6 eV and 192.8 eV (Fig. S3a), which are associated with C–B and B–N bonds, respectively (Chen et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2014b). As depicted in Fig. 1f, one peak in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum indicated a binding energy of 283.7 eV for the C–B bond, which further confirmed that the boron atoms were present in the r-CDs. Overall, the corresponding XPS and FT-IR spectra further illustrated that the presence of nitrogen, phosphorus, and boron atoms and various chemical bonds were perfect in the r-CDs. The optical properties of r-CDs may change appreciably because of the introduction of nitrogen, phosphorus, and boron atoms (Huang et al., 2018; Martindale et al., 2017). In addition, the zeta potentials of the r-CDs and the pristine CDs were characterized (Fig. S4d). Compared with the pristine CDs, the potential of r-CDs reduced, which further demonstrates the success of the preparation of r-CDs (Xu et al., 2013).

Furthermore, the optical properties of r-CDs were characterized. An evident absorption peak of r-CDs was observed from 300 nm to 350 nm (Fig. 2 a), which was attributed to the
π −
π ∗
transition of the double bonds (Peng et al., 2016). Compared with the unreduced CDs (Fig. S5), the UV absorption peak of the r-CDs changed. Both the excitation and emission peaks underwent blue shifting (380/480 nm, 320/450 nm), causing the fluorescence to change from cyan to blue (Fig. 2a), which confirmed that the boron element was modified at the surface of r-CDs (Guo et al., 2021). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2b, the emission of r-CDs showed an excitation-dependent behavior attributed to the effect of surface states on the bandgap of r-CDs (Gong et al., 2016b; Pacheco-Linan et al., 2020). The band spacing of r-CDs is affected by the complexity of the excited states of r-CDs, largely stemming from their particle sizes and surface states (Luo et al., 2019; Strauss et al., 2014). The observable excitation and emission peaks (320/450 nm) were also reflected in the 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2c). Moreover, quinine sulfate was used as a reference, and the relative QY of the r-CDs was estimated to be 18.8% (Figure S7a and S7b). To investigate FL intensity stability, the experiments for fluorescence scans of 7 d and 1 h shown in Fig. 2d and S6 were carried out, which demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity can be maintained. Overall, r-CDs can be selected as a promising material for practical environmental pollutant monitoring owing to their superior optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of TC based on the r-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence sensing experiments based on r-CDs detecting TC were performed. Fluorescence measurements were performed after 2 min of reaction with different concentrations of TC (0–600 μM) and blended with 300 μg/mL r-CDs in PBS buffer solution (pH = 10) at 25 °C (Fig. S8). The FL intensity at 450 nm gradually decreased with increasing TC concentration (Fig. 3 a), indicating that the blue fluorescence of the r-CDs was progressively quenched. The fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) was highly proportional to the range of TC concentration (0–150 μM, R2 = 0.99767) with an estimated limit of detection (LOD) of 1.73 nM (Fig. 3b). The LOD of TC was calculated according to the following Eq. (3) (Long et al., 2021b):
(3)
LOD =
K
S b
m
where K is 3, S
b is the standard deviation for three repeated fluorescence measurements of the r-CDs solution in the absence of TC, and m denotes the slope of the curve.

Selectivity and anti-interference experiments for TC detection were also conducted to replicate the complexity of the wastewater environment. Common metal cations (Hg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Li+), and various antibiotics (TC, CTE, OXY, AZM, CHL, AMX, LEX, and STR) were added at 100 μM. The red bars in Fig. 3c and d indicate the fluorescence signal of the r-CDs to the external coexisting substance, with subsequent addition of 100 μM TC to every solution (blue bars, Fig. 3c and d). The FL intensity changed significantly only with the addition of TC. Moreover, the results of other concentration ratios (TC/anti-interference substances) were consistent in all tests (Fig. S11). These results indicate that the coexisting substances have little effect on the TC of r-CDs, suggesting favorable specificity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensing of TC based on the r-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To further explore the colorimetric response of r-CDs detecting TC, controlled experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the mixed solutions were scanned at 320–650 nm with a UV spectrophotometer, and there was an evident absorption peak at 537 nm of r-CDs after adding TC, indicating that compared with the pristine CDs, the presence of r-CDs solely contributes to the colorimetric detection of TC. In addition, the influence of excessive NaBH4 can be ruled out (Fig. 4a), which further shows that r-CDs are the active substance for the colorimetric monitoring of TC. Different concentrations of TC (0–600 μM) were mixed with 300 μg/mL r-CDs and PBS buffer solution of pH = 10, and incubated at 25 °C for 70 min (Fig. S9). The color of the mixture changed from colorless to red after the continuous addition of TC, which could be easily observed with the naked eye (inset in Fig. 4c). The absorbance spectra of r-CDs in the presence of different TC concentrations are shown in Fig. 4c. The absorption peak of the mixed solution was observed to appear at 537 nm and gradually increased with the addition of TC. Notably, the calibration curve of absorbance versus the concentration of TC changed linearly from 0 to 150 μM (R2 = 0.9951), and the LOD was 0.46 μM (Fig. 4d). The LOD of TC was calculated according to Eq. (4):
(4)
LOD =
K
S b
m
where K is 3, S
b is the standard deviation for three repeated absorbance measurements of the r-CDs solution in the absence of TC, and m denotes the slope of the curve.

Similarly, due to the complex and diverse water composition, selectivity and anti-interference experiments for this mode of TC detection were also conducted. We tested the absorption variations of r-CDs versus TC in the presence of various possible coexisting cations, such as various heavy metal ions (Hg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, and Li+), and various antibiotics (TC, CTE, OXY, AZM, CHL, AMX, LEX, STR, and DOX) at 100 μM. As shown in Fig. 4e and f, the red bars represent the absorbance response of r-CDs to the common metal ions, and the blue bars indicate the absorbance of the above solution with the addition of 100 μM TC. These results indicate that heavy metal ions and tetracycline antibiotics have little interference effect on r-CDs detecting TC. As depicted in Fig. S12, the results of different ratios of TC/interference content further confirmed that the interferents had no obvious influence on sensing TC. Thus, r-CDs can be used for sensing TC in the colorimetric mode, while exhibiting good sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the detection to TC of r-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The characteristics of FRET are mainly derived under two conditions. First, the fluorescence lifetime can decrease, and second, the Förster effective radius is normally 1–10 nm between the two ligands (Liang et al., 2016; Miao et al., 2018). The fluorescence quenching response of r-CDs to TC corresponded with the Stern–Volmer equation (Fig. 5 a). The Stern–Volmer quenching constants (Ksv) were calculated using Eq. (5) (Shangguan et al., 2017):
(5)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
⋅ C
where F0 and F are the FL signals in the absence and presence of TC, respectively, the concentration of TC is represented by C, and the Stern–Volmer quenching constant is KSV. The plot of F0/F against C shows a proportional relationship from 0.001 to 600 μM with a KSV of 1.310 × 104 M−1.

The emission spectrum of the r-CDs was overlaid with the absorption spectrum of TC (Fig. 5b). The decay curves of the r-CDs in the absence and presence of TC were then analyzed (Fig. 5c). As shown in the table inset in Fig. 5c, the average fluorescence lifetime without TC was 7.60 ns (450 nm). With the addition of 100 μM TC, the average lifetime of r-CDs was estimated to be 5.80 ns (450 nm), indicating that the FRET process may have occurred between r-CDs and TC. Further analysis on the mechanism of FRET between them, the energy transfer efficiency (E) was calculated using Eq. (6). From this, the r-CDs the Förster effective radius (r) and Förster distance (R0) between the r-CDs and TC can be estimated by Eqs. (6)–(9) (Chatzimarkou et al., 2018; Yan et al., 2018).
(6)
E = 1 −
τ 0
τ
(7)
R 0
= 0.211
[
K 2
n
− 4
∅ J
( λ )
]
1 6
(8)
E =
1
[ 1 +
(
r
R 0
)
6
]
(9)
J
( λ )
=
∫
0 ∞
F D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )
λ 4
d λ

Here,
τ 0
and
τ
are the lifetimes before and after the addition of TC, respectively. K is the orientation factor of the r-CDs and the quencher transition dipoles are assumed to be 2/3,
∅
is the quantum yield of the r-CDs (Fig. S7b), n is the refractive index of the medium (generally 1.33), and
J
( λ )
is the integral of the overlap values.
F D
( λ )
is the corrected FL intensity of the r-CDs in the range of
λ
to
λ + Δ λ
with the total intensity normalized to unity, and
ε A
( λ
) is the extinction coefficient of the TC at
λ
in M−1 cm−1. The Förster effective radius was calculated to be between 1 nm and 10 nm. Therefore, all results indicate that FRET evolution occurs between r-CDs and TC.

To investigate the sensing mechanism for the colorimetric detection of TC, characterization experiments were carried out, including zeta potential and FT-IR. Compared with other antibiotics, a new peak of the r-CDs solution appeared at 537 nm with the addition of TC, stemming from a complicated interaction between r-CDs and TC (Fig. 5a). Furthermore, the mixed solution showed color changes in the presence of TC (inset in Fig. 5a). As shown in Fig. 1c, there were hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH), and amino (–NH4) groups in the r-CDs, and the zeta potential of the r-CDs was measured to be −13.6 mV due to the presence –COOH and –OH on the surface of r-CDs. (Fig. S4d). After the addition of TC, the zeta potential was recorded to be −15.9 mV. Provided that –OH and –COOH bonded to TC, the potential could be more positive. Hence, the positive potential of –NH4 was partly inhibited, indicating that TC was mostly coordinated with multiple –NH4 (Liu et al., 2017). In contrast to the FT-IR spectrum and high-resolution N 1s of r-CDs before and after the addition of TC, this possible explanation was further explored. With the addition of TC, C–N bonds significantly increased (Fig. 5e, f, and Fig. 1f), further illustrating that TC selectively formed a complex coordination with the –NH4 of r-CDs (Liu et al., 2017, 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Dual-mode sensing based on r-CDs was used for the detection of TC in water samples. The water sample was pretreated before analysis and the raw water sample was measured at about 0.5 μM. The treated water samples were then added to different standard concentrations of TC (Table 1 ). The formula for calculating the recovery rate was as follows:
(10)
P =
C 2
−
C 1
C 3
where C1 is the TC concentration of the water sample, C2 is the measured TC concentration of the sample after adding the standard TC concentrations, and C3 is the standard TC concentration.

The monitoring results of TC in real samples, summarized in Table 1, demonstrate an excellent recovery range (98%–102%) and high detection sensitivity. These results provide reliability and feasibility for the dual-mode detection of TC based on the r-CDs nanosensor in water samples. Comparing the capability of the synthesized r-CDs with previous studies (Table S1), the synthesized r-CDs have advantages in terms of sensitivity and selectivity in fluorescence and colorimetry.

In summary, novel N, P, and B co-doped reduced carbon dots (r-CDs) were prepared by simple hydrothermal treatment and chemical redox reactions. The as-synthesized r-CDs exhibited favorable chemical properties, high quantum yields (18.8%), and fluorescent stability. Dual-mode sensing based on r-CDs for TC detection was developed with LODs of 1.73 nM for fluorescence and 0.46 μM for colorimetry, respectively. More importantly, the fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode nanosensors have been successfully used to detect TC in wastewater, which may aid in the detection of TC in swine wastewater, antibiotic drug factory wastewater, and agricultural runoff, providing a sensitive, rapid, visible, and on-spot analytical platform for real environmental pollutants. The strategy proposed in this work may offer a unique idea for designing dual-mode sensing mechanisms for different detection requirements based on stimuli-responsive nanomaterials.

Qing Fu: Conceptualization; Investigation; Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing. Caicheng Long: Investigation; Writing - original draft. Lingfeng Qin: Writing - review & editing. Zixin Jiang: Writing - review & editing. Taiping Qing: Writing - review & editing. Peng Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Bo Feng: Project administration; Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 21777135, 51708475) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 2019JJ40283, 2019JJ50596, 2018JJ3496), the Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department, China (19A501, 18C0102), and Hunan 2011 Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Engineering & Technology with Environmental Benignity and Effective Resource Utilization.
